Q1: I tried to set up a consistent routine, honestly I did. I'd block out about 25 hours a week for studying, mostly in the evenings when my roommates were quieter. I learn best by listening, so I'd play lecture recordings while taking notes, sometimes rewinding parts multiple times. But the routine kept falling apart because I was dealing with a lot of stress and sadness that made it hard to stick to plans.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on how I was feeling emotionally on any given day. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram intensely, but during quieter weeks I'd sometimes go days without touching course material. The stress I was under made consistency really difficult, even though I knew regular study would help me more in the long run. I wanted to be disciplined but my mental state kept interfering.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just scrolling through without really engaging. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because earning points or badges gave me small wins when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q4: For a few weeks mid-semester, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic for me. The gamified elements helped with that—I'd want to see if I'd earned anything or if there were new challenges. But then things got harder emotionally and that routine slipped away. It never fully became second nature the way checking my phone is.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the concepts that way. The discussion forums were also helpful because reading what other students were confused about made me feel less alone in struggling. The gamified tasks gave me structure when I couldn't motivate myself otherwise.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and PDF documents they uploaded. Reading dense text when I'm stressed just doesn't work for my brain—the words blur together and nothing sticks. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but later never came. Some of the supplementary resources also felt redundant if the lecture already covered the same content.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams, or if it gave me that sense of accomplishment through the gamified system. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly explained just added to my stress. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent wisely because I didn't have much to spare emotionally.
Q8: Required activities carried this weight of anxiety—I had to do them or face consequences, which sometimes paralyzed me more than motivated me. Optional activities through the gamified system actually felt lighter, more like choices I was making for myself. Ironically, I sometimes engaged more with optional gamified tasks because they didn't trigger my anxiety the same way mandatory deadlines did.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while walking or lying in bed when I couldn't face sitting at my desk. I also found some YouTube videos that explained concepts in different ways that clicked better for me. My notes from listening were extensive but none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole evenings where I'd listen to downloaded lectures on repeat, pausing to think through problems, working through concepts in my head. I'd be genuinely learning but my LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in. Sometimes my best studying happened when I was too sad to look at screens but could still listen and process information.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a lecture, watch it once online to mark it as viewed, then listen to it offline three or four more times over the following days. The LMS only captured that initial view, not the hours I spent with that same material afterward. This was especially true for difficult topics that needed multiple passes to understand.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed for me emotionally. The stress became unbearable and I felt this deep sadness that made getting out of bed difficult, let alone engaging with coursework. My attendance dropped because I couldn't face being around people. Even logging into the LMS felt like too much some days—the interface just reminded me of how behind I was falling.
Q13: The high stress absolutely destroyed my ability to use the LMS consistently. When I was overwhelmed, I'd sometimes open the platform and just stare at it without clicking anything, feeling paralyzed. Fatigue from poor sleep made concentrating on videos nearly impossible. I had to choose between assignments for different courses, and this one often lost that competition.
Q14: Many times I'd watch videos but realize twenty minutes in that I hadn't absorbed anything—my mind was elsewhere, cycling through anxious thoughts. I'd be technically present, logged in, video playing, but mentally I was somewhere else entirely. The LMS would record engagement that wasn't really happening. It's a hollow kind of activity that looks productive but isn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd think about all the ways I could fail and freeze up. Sometimes the sadness was so heavy that I'd choose sleep over studying just to escape how I was feeling. I knew I was hurting myself but couldn't break the pattern.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of panic-driven activity. I'd suddenly log in multiple times a day as a deadline approached, cramming everything into the last possible hours. My assignment scores were actually good when I submitted—94.6%—but I just didn't submit enough of them. The deadline pressure was both motivating and paralyzing depending on my mental state that day.
Q17: When I got good feedback on the assignments I did submit, it gave me a temporary boost—proof that I could do this if I just engaged. But that feeling faded quickly under the weight of everything else. Negative feedback or seeing low exam scores made me want to avoid the course entirely. The gamified feedback was gentler somehow, less crushing than traditional grades.
Q18: The course structure assumed a level of consistency I couldn't maintain. Weekly expectations piled up when I missed one, creating this snowball effect of falling behind. The gamified elements tried to break things into smaller pieces, which helped when I could engage with them. But the overall structure didn't account for students who might be struggling with more than just the content.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier by giving me small, achievable goals when everything else felt insurmountable. But the attendance requirements were brutal for someone dealing with what I was dealing with—10% attendance reflects how hard it was to physically show up. The course design assumed students could be present regularly, which wasn't my reality.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat. I was logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, doing the gamified activities—but my actual assignment completion was terrible. The gap between engagement and output can happen when someone is struggling to convert their learning into submitted work. Mental health issues can create exactly this disconnect.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of my situation, but I understand it. Some students are efficient—they know exactly what they need, get it done quickly, and don't need to spend hours in the system. I spent a lot of time in the LMS without it translating to success because my engagement was often unfocused or anxiety-driven rather than productive.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually learning or just going through motions while my mind was elsewhere. They definitely can't capture the studying I did offline or the emotional state that shaped everything I did or didn't do.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning process. It's not just logging in or watching videos—it's when your mind is present and you're genuinely trying to grow. For me, real engagement was rare this semester because my emotional state kept pulling me away even when I wanted to be present.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to stay afloat. It can't capture the tears of frustration, the hours lying awake anxious about falling behind, or the genuine effort I put into the assignments I actually submitted. My high assignment scores prove I understood the material—my low completion rate shows I was drowning in something the data can't see. I'm a climber, but this semester the mountain was too steep.
